title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | AKC suspends breeders for life The Associated Press GREAT FALLS, Mont. (July 15, 6:05 p.m. ADT) - The American Kennel Club's board of directors has suspended convicted collie abusers Jon and Athena Harman from ever registering dogs or entering dogs in AKC shows. The AKC ordinarily suspends breeders convicted of animal cruelty for 10 years and fines them $2,000. But because of the number of dogs involved "and the horrific conditions these dogs were made to endure," the organization's Management Disciplinary Committee recommended a lifetime suspension for the Harmans. The AKC governs all reputable dog shows and its stamp of approval is considered essential to American breeders. At the same time it handed out the suspensions, the board reduced its usual $2,000 fine for convicted animal …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Bears are a worry, but sting of cow parsnip likelier woe (Published: July 20, 2003) Cow parsnip in the woods near Goose Lake. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge UPPER RUSSIAN RIVER TRAIL -- With the ground hidden beneath a 6-foot wall of vegetation, we rode on, nervously shouting "Yo, bear!" now and again. But it wasn't the bears that made us nervous. There were bears about, but the chances of encountering one did not appear high. Of far greater concern was the danger we could see all around: Alaska cow parsnip, or Heracleum lanatum as it is botanically classified, was everywhere. If you have yet to have a nasty encounter with this Alaska plant, just wait. It will come -- the itching, the burning, the oozing from the blisters, the blood from where…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Native corporation intends to drill ASRC: BP Exploration to share technical knowledge, data with company. By KAY CASHMAN Petroleum News (Published: July 14, 2003) Arctic Slope Regional Corp., a company representing the business interests of 9,000 Inupiat Eskimos, is expanding its scope to become an independent oil and gas producer in its own backyard -- the oil-rich North Slope. As the next step in its evolution from oil field service provider and land owner/manager, ASRC has entered into a "mentoring" agreement with major North Slope producer BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc., the state's largest investor and one of the biggest oil and gas companies in the world. The agreement "establishes a framework for sharing data and technical knowledge" between the two companies, ASRC and BP compan…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Seward slaying sets off manhunt SEARCH: Authorities set up roadblocks, comb woods for suspect. By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 21, 2003) Bill Conger is suspected of shooting co-worker John Tulin in Seward, state troopers say. Click on photo to enlarge John Tulin, 43, and Bill Conger, 31, were co-workers and friends in Seward. But Saturday night, Tulin was found shot dead in his driveway and Conger is suspected of doing the killing, Alaska State Troopers said. Tulin lived with his wife and daughter in a trailer in the Questa Woods Subdivision near Mile 6 of the Seward Highway. He was shot with a large caliber rifle, trooper Sgt. Brandon Anderson said. Someone called in the shooting at 8:57 p.m. Saturday. Troopers think Conger walked into nearby woods. They …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska's pot law invalid, lawyer claims CONSTITUTION: Supreme Court ruling trumps 1990 ban, he argues. The Associated Press (Published: July 21, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- The Alaska Court of Appeals is considering a case that could set precedent in the 1975 Alaska Supreme Court ruling protecting marijuana possession for personal use in the home. A lawyer for a North Pole man convicted in 2001 of possessing marijuana in his home has appealed the conviction based on a claim that a nearly three-decade-old Alaska Supreme Court decision declaring personal pot possession a state constitutional right is still the law. The Court of Appeals' decision will represent the first time a precedent-setting court decides whether the Supreme Court ruling still protects marijuana possession for personal use i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Damage repaired, liner sails SUMMIT: 2,000 passengers leave Seward after two-day delay. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 14, 2003) Repair crews on Sunday finished sealing a large hole in a cruise ship's hull, and the U.S. Coast Guard gave its captain the go-ahead to sail out of Seward, where the vessel had been stranded since early Friday morning. The Celebrity Cruises ship Summit was damaged last Wednesday after hitting a rock as it was leaving Yakutat Bay after visiting Hubbard Glacier. The rock tore a 10-foot-long hole in the starboard side of its hull. The part of the hull that was damaged covered a ballast tank that can be filled with water to stabilize the ship. Divers spent two days repairing the breach, creating a temporary fix that will stand until …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Wayward tern finds its way to Fairbanks RARE SIGHT: Bird spotted in flats usually seen only in Asia and Europe. The Associated Press (Published: July 17, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- Bird lovers flocked to flats south of Fairbanks this week to view a rare visitor: a white-winged tern usually seen only in Asia and Europe. A white-winged tern was first reported in Alaska on July 12, 1976. Twenty-seven years later to the day, on Saturday, bird watchers saw another. Its visit marked just the fourth recorded sighting in the state. "That's the unusual thing about birds. They're so mobile, one can get caught in a storm or have its internal compass screwed up and show up anywhere it the world," said John Wright, a Department of Fish and Game biologist who viewed the bird. Florida resident Jeff Bouton …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | State draws male nurses, students CAREER: Alaska lifestyle suggested as reason for high numbers. The Associated Press (Published: July 14, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- Former paramedic Alan Freytag started his nursing career nine years ago as a way to have a closer relationship with his patients. "I found that when I would take my people to the emergency room in the ambulance, I wanted to stay with them," said Freytag, who works in the intensive care unit at Fairbanks Memorial Hospital. Freytag, 46, is among the growing ranks of male nurses in the state and nation. In Alaska, the percentage of male nurses and men enrolled in nursing school programs is above the national average, according to Dr. Tina DeLapp, director of the School of Nursing at the University of Alaska Anchorage. In 2000, 7.6 …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | National bill would boost ferry funds $150 MILLION: Senator's proposal quadruples feds' share. McClatchy News Service (Published: June 29, 2003) WASHINGTON -- Federal funding for ferries, including those operating in Alaska, would quadruple to $150 million a year under legislation introduced Thursday by Sen. Patty Murray, D-Wash. Murray's bill has support from senators representing other states with major ferry systems, and the measure could eventually become a part of the six-year transportation-spending plan Congress is expected to adopt this year. Last year, the nation's six largest ferry systems -- in Alaska, Washington, Hawaii, New York-New Jersey, North Carolina and San Francisco -- carried 73 million people and 13 million vehicles. Washington state has the largest ferry system…

summary: | | | Community Datebook (Published: July 17, 2003) BLOOD BANK The Wal-Mart blood drive will be noon-7 p.m. Friday at the store at 3101 A St.; appointments can be made at 222-5636; walk-ins will be accepted as time permits. Blood Bank of Alaska has ongoing needs. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | In Brief (Published: July 11, 2003) Families urged to join in Healthy Families Day Gov. Frank Murkowski issued a proclamation in April naming July 12 Healthy Families Day in line with the goals of the local group Parents and Children Together. In celebration of the day, families are encouraged to set aside time Saturday to share a meal, go on a hike or otherwise spend time together. PACT's mission is to encourage families to strengthen ties between parents and children through communication and activities. For more information about PACT, call Yolanda Clary, 278-3250 or e-mail yolandacc@yahoo.com . Recycle your glass at Brown Jug Warehouse Alaskans for Litter Prevention and Recycling have installed drop-off bins for glass, which is recycled locally, at Brown Jug Warehouse on the Old …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska Digest (Published: July 9, 2003) ANCHORAGE Community council boundary changes get preliminary approval The Anchorage Assembly on Tuesday gave preliminary approval to changes in city community council boundaries that would maintain the large Northeast Community Council but expand North Star south from Fireweed Avenue to 36th Avenue. The boundary changes are the first since the municipality created community councils in 1975. Boundaries are supposed to undergo review every two years. The city planning department will change maps to reflect changes the Assembly approved Tuesday and present them to the Assembly for final approval at its July 22 meeting. Changes approved Tuesday also split the Powder Reserve in Eagle River between the Birchwood and Eagle River community councils an…

summary: | | | Community Datebook (Published: July 8, 2003) TODAY COMMUNITY An all-terrain vehicle safety meeting will be conducted by the U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission, 9 a.m.-5 p.m. at Loussac Library, 3600 Denali St. "We want to hear from Alaskans -- how they use their ATVs -- and get their perspective on ATV safety issues," said the commission's chairman, Hal Stratton, who will conduct the meeting. Requests to make oral presentations at the meeting may be made to Lizzy Gary by phone at 1-301-504-7908, by fax at 1-301-504-0768, or e-mail to egary@cpsc.gov . title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | New online courses aid tiny schools 21 CHOICES: Learning method teaches self-reliance and computer skills. By ERIC FRY The Juneau Empire (Published: June 30, 2003) JUNEAU -- Alaska educators hope new online courses will help students, especially in small rural schools, meet state standards and be prepared for college. The courses also will help schools obey new federal requirements to use highly qualified teachers. Alaska Online, a consortium of nine school districts, has been offering a pilot program of 21 courses for high school students for a year and will start officially this fall. Alaska teachers developed most of the courses, many of which are in English and math. Alaska Online officials said the students will be enrolled in regular schools but need courses that aren't offered…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | He got Lance on wheeled way FIRST COACH: Bike shop owner sold 7-year-old Armstrong a bicycle. By ROY NEESE Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 23, 2003) ROY NEESE / Anchorage Daily News Jim Hoyt, owner of Richardson Bike Mart in a Dallas suburb, sold a bike to 7-year-old Lance Armstrong nearly 25 years ago and became his first coach. Now Armstrong is racing for his fifth straight win in the Tour de France, cycling's premier long-distance event. Armstrong highlighted Hoyt's influence on his career in his book, "It's Not About the Bike." Click on photo to enlarge RICHARDSON, Texas -- Jim Hoyt has sold a lot of bicycles. One, though, still stands out. "Schwinn mag scrambler. Bronzy, with red wheels." The buyer? A 7-year-old from an apartment complex across the street. Nearly 25 years …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Total to share NPR-A drilling risk PARTNERS: French oil giant brings in a Canadian firm for exploration well. By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 22, 2003) The U.S. subsidiary of French oil giant Total has brought in a Canadian partner for its exploratory drilling plans in the National Petroleum Reserve-Alaska. Such partnerships are normal in the Alaska oil patch and globally, said Jack Bergeron, Alaska manager for Total E&P USA Inc. of Houston, Texas. "You share the risk," he said. "We have partnerships with various companies throughout the world. Sometimes you partner to gain expertise, but in this case it's to share risk." Fortuna Exploration, a subsidiary of Talisman Energy Inc., based in Calgary, Alberta, signed an agreement to join Total in exploring land on the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | A day so perfect for canoeing, some can't bear thought of clothes (Published: July 23, 2003) "It's the best day of the year so far," the naked man said as he paddled his canoe in the Saturday sunshine on Owl Lake, northwest of Wasilla. With that, the slender fellow with the all-over tan smiled easily and paddled off. He was about 50 and wore nothing except a cap and sunglasses. The man seemed to be as much a part of the series of lakes and forested shores in the Nancy Lake State Recreation Area as the lily pads, the loons and his exquisite wooden canoe. We had arrived at James Lake on Friday evening and encountered him the next day, on a bugless afternoon. While we chatted with a family of four whom we met on the shore of Char Lake at the end of a portage, the man hung back on the la…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Suspended doctor faces fraud, other charges HOME SALE: Two are accused of falsifying the sale of a home. By NICOLE TSONG Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 23, 2003) The state medical board in February upheld the suspension of Dr. Samuel Schurig's license. Click on photo to enlarge An Eagle River doctor has been accused of fraud and conspiracy in federal court less than a year after he had his license suspended by the Alaska State Medical Board. Dr. Samuel Schurig and Delynn Brouillet, a loan officer for a mortgage corporation, were indicted by a federal grand jury last week on charges that they falsified the sale of a home to obtain a loan from the federal Department of Housing and Urban Development. They both face one charge of conspiracy and two charges of HUD fraud. Schurig, 5…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Troopers arrest suspect in shooting death CROWN POINT: Bill Conger was found after three-day search. By TATABOLINE BRANT Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 23, 2003) Bill Conger is suspected of killing his friend John Tulin. Click on photo to enlarge The hunt for the man suspected of killing his friend near Seward over the weekend came to an end Tuesday night with an arrest along the railroad tracks near Crown Point. Alaska State Troopers said they found Bill Conger, 31, walking along the tracks near Mile 22.5 Seward Highway just before 9 p.m. Troopers had been searching for Conger around the clock for three days in the densely wooded area north of Seward. Conger was taken into custody without incident, said Capt. Tom Bowman. He said Conger knew he was being pursued and had been o…

By TATABOLINE BRANT Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 22, 2003) Shooting suspect Bill Conger worked with his friend John Tulin at Auction Block. Click on photo to enlarge The hunt for the man suspected of killing his close friend over the weekend continued in a densely wooded area near Seward, but as of Monday night, avid outdoorsman Bill Conger had not been found, Alaska State Troopers said. Conger, 31, is suspected of killing John Tulin, his 43-year-old friend and co-worker, troopers said. Tulin was found shot to death with a large-caliber rifle in his driveway on Saturday night, troopers said. Tulin lived with his wife and daughter in a trailer in the Questa Woods Subdivision just outside S…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Pilot gave no indication of fuel problems By SEAN COCKERHAM Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 22, 2003) JUNEAU -- A Utah pilot gave air traffic controllers no indication of fuel problems until just before his plane took its fatal plunge into Icy Strait, according to a National Transportation Safety Board investigation. "There was no mention at all, all the way up the line," said Clint Johnson, who is leading the crash investigation for the NTSB. That is the finding of a preliminary accident report that the NTSB expects to release today Johnson said. The report is to be largely based on a review of the communications that the pilot, Gary Ostler, had with air traffic controllers all along the plane's route from Port Angeles, Wash., up through the panhandle of Southeast Alaska. Six …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Girdwood hiker battles, races bears BLACKIES: Attacking the animals is a good idea, but not if they're brown. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 20, 2003) Armed only with a big stick, U.S. Forest Service recreation technician Jeff Nissman last week waged a successful, hour-long, running battle with a black bear along the California Creek Trail in the Girdwood Valley. Over the course of the encounter, Nissman repeatedly clubbed the bear. That drove it back, but did not curb its interest. The bear, according to Nissman, refused to give up the chase until the man reached the regularly trafficked Crow Creek Road. "That was pretty odd,'' area wildlife biologist Rick Sinnott of the Alaska Department of Fish and Game said later. But not unprecedented. The bear behavior Ni…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Bristol Bay land to join lease sale EXPLORATION: Hard times in fishing prompt residents' support. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 3, 2003) The Alaska Division of Oil and Gas will open up millions of acres around Bristol Bay to exploration, adding them to the state's five-year leasing program, Gov. Frank Murkowski said Wednesday. The area comprises state and Native corporation land. It was added to the lease program at the request of the Bristol Bay Native Corp., the governor said. Local residents for years have been reluctant to support oil and gas development because of concerns about the potential environmental impact on the region's salmon fisheries, an important part of the Bristol Bay economy. But with the recent downturn in the fishing industry, many …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Dad sues state for keeping daughter COMPLAINT: Agency kept raising bar before he could regain custody. By LISA DEMER Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 16, 2003) A Nikiski father has accused the state's child-protection agency of lying, violating his rights and keeping him from his young daughter for nearly two years without good reason. Vernon Smith, a Cook Inlet oil worker who goes by "Skip," regained his daughter more than a year ago, in April 2002. Now he's suing the state Department of Health and Social Services, its former Division of Family and Youth Services and two workers. The civil lawsuit filed earlier this month in Kenai Superior Court asks a judge to determine that DFYS wronged Smith and his family. He is asking for more than $100,000 in damages. The real goal of the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Another scorcher sets another daily heat record 82 DEGREES: Since July 4, Anchorage has seen only one day that didn't reach 70. By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 15, 2003) Once again Monday, the city basked in record-setting warmth. For the second day in a row and the third time in seven days, the official daily high for Anchorage set a record, according to National Weather Service figures. The official high Monday, reached just after 4 p.m., was 82 degrees, shattering the former record for July 14 of 77 degrees, set in 1935, the agency said. Sunday's high of 77 degrees bested the previous record of 76, while the high on July 8 of 84 degrees set not only a daily record of 78 but also the all-time monthly high for July, knocking off a 50-year-old mark, the Weather S…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Rare storm endangers McKinley climbers 35 IN PATH: Wind ripping through Denali Pass could reach 120 mph. By DOUG O'HARRA Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 15, 2003) A rare summer storm will spin through northern Alaska today and Wednesday, dusting snow across the Brooks Range and coastal villages and hammering the season's last climbers on Mount McKinley with dangerous winds, the National Weather Service said. The agency warned of 40 to 80 mph winds, with blasts hitting 120 mph in Denali Pass by today. The pass separates the north and south peaks of McKinley. Climbing rangers in Talkeetna relayed the forecast by cell phone to several groups still on the mountain over the weekend, according to staffers with Denali National Park. "It's fairly rare for the state to see this in the m…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Digital ducks Four birds to get devices that will test satellite tracking By DOUG O'HARRA Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 14, 2003) Common Eider ducklings at the Alaska SeaLife Center hatched between June 22 and June 27. (Photo courtesy of Alaska SeaLife Center ) Click on photo to enlarge F our fuzzy ducklings hatched during the past few weeks at the Alaska SeaLife Center will ultimately test the accuracy of orbiting satellites and demonstrate how seabirds fare with miniature computers placed inside their bodies. The three female and one male common eiders -- nicknamed Solstice, June, Yukon and Summer -- were born in the five-day period beginning June 22 at the Seward research facility, said eider research technician Bill O'Connell. The birds came from eggs gathered on Kigigak …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Judge: Estate looted SCHEME: Kenai doctor denies stealing $6 million from dead mother's fortune. By SHEILA TOOMEY Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 14, 2003) Cole Click on photo to enlarge A civil judgment in excess of $6 million, issued by an Anchorage Superior Court judge, accuses a Kenai doctor of looting her dead mother's estate in a money laundering scheme that involved impersonation, forged checks and off-shore bank accounts. "The Court finds that Elisabeth Cole is perhaps one of the most corrosive and destructive persons that this Court has had the unfortunate opportunity to run into," says the judgment, filed June 26 by Judge Sen Tan. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Museum throws 35th birthday party STILL GROWING: A third expansion is on the horizon for facility. By S. JANE SZABO Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 13, 2003) If a building could move, the Anchorage Museum of History and Art would take a bow today. If it could hear, it would hear the word "Congratulations!" echoing through its halls. And if it could smile, it would grin from archives to atrium. Instead, longtime supporters like Pat Wolf will be doing the honors for the museum on the occasion of its 35th anniversary. "Birthdays and anniversaries are just nice times to look back, to look to the present and to look forward," Wolf, the museum's director, said in a phone interview last week. Looking backward, we see the photo of the museum's 1967 ground breaking, with Elmer and Mary …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Pilot dies in crash near Kachemak Bay ENGLISH BAY: Homer-based Cessna 206 hits water after aborted landing. Daily News staff and wire reports (Published: July 12, 2003) A Smokey Bay Air pilot died Friday morning when a company Cessna 206 crashed on takeoff into English Bay near Homer. Stephen Finley, 49, of Hopewell, N.J., was piloting the craft and was alone when it crashed about 11 a.m., Alaska State Troopers said. Troopers in Homer received a 911 call about the crash about 11 a.m. Troopers, including dive team members, responded as did the Coast Guard, which dispatched a helicopter from Kodiak. The cutter Hickory sent out a small boat with rescuers. The plane went down in about 25 feet of water, said Petty Officer Darrell Wilson, a Coast Guard spokesman. Troopers recovered the bod…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | State dioceses face audit, survey ABUSE: One project will monitor compliance with new Catholic rules, other determines extent of problem. By RACHEL D'ORO The Associated Press (Published: July 12, 2003) The three Catholic dioceses in Alaska are taking part in two national sexual-abuse projects to gauge the level of compliance to new church norms and to determine the extent of abuse over the past 50 years, state Catholic leaders said Friday. All 195 dioceses in the United States are being audited to see if their policies conform with national guidelines regarding sexual abuse of children that were adopted at a Roman Catholic bishops conference in Dallas last year. All dioceses also are participating in the past-abuse survey. Final reports on the projects will be made public after appro…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Fairview rape may be hate crime TEEN: White victim said perpetrators made comments about race. By TATABOLINE BRANT Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 12, 2003) Police said Friday they are investigating as a hate crime the beating and rape of a 17-year-old girl who was attacked by a group of men in a field on the edge of Fairview. Three men were involved in the assault, police said. One man has already been arrested. Police put out a description Friday for a second man they want to question. Deputy Police Chief Bill Miller said the case is being investigated as a possible hate crime because police believe the men made comments about race to the victim when they raped her. Miller refused to say what the comments were, as it could jeopardize the investigation, he said. The victim, wh…

During a news conference in Anchorage on Friday, state Attorney General Gregg Renkes delivers his office's opinion about how income to the Permanent Fund is to be calculated to determine dividends. Buy the Canadian edition of "Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix." The Canadian publisher, working with conservation groups, has produced a forest-friendly edition that conservationists are describing as a way to protect boreal forests, an important habitat for North American songbirds and waterfowl. Author J.K. Rowling writes that the Canadian Harry Potter books are "helping to save magnificent creatures in the muggle world, forests that are home of magical animals such as orangutans, wolves and bears." She adds, "It's a good idea to respect ancient trees, especially if th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | After breathing poetry for 67 years, writer wins laurels OCTOGENARIAN: Kenai man gets two awards from Famous Poets Society. 'Bunkhouse in the Sky' The Associated Press (Published: July 2, 2003) KENAI -- William Rowe wrote his first poem when he was 17 and stuck in a hospital with the flu. He got bored, so he started writing. The poem had a military theme, and when an Army commander passed through the hospital and saw it, he told Rowe he would have it published. Now, after 67 years of writing poetry, Rowe in May won his first prizes in a poetry contest, the Shakespeare Trophy of Excellence and the Poet of the Year Medallion for 2002, from the Famous Poets Society. Poetry for Rowe is part of who he is. "It's not something I have a desire for; it's something that has to be. I have to writ…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska's economy on the verge of a downturn DAVID REAUME COMMENT (Published: June 29, 2003) Alaskans are economic optimists and have been for as long as I can remember. In part that is because people who are not so optimistic pack up and move elsewhere but also in part because statewide employment has been on a 16-year up trend. Fewer than half of today's Alaska residents were around the last time the Alaska economy saw a year-to-year drop in employment. Of those who were around, many fewer than half personally experienced job loss or the agony of turning over one's house keys to the mortgage holder. The possibility that somehow employment will do anything but grow may be only an abstract concept to all but a few current Alaska residents. To be exact, it has been 16 years since nonagri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Delta residents irked by Army training site plan EDDY DROP ZONE: People think Army's planned site too close. The Associated Press (Published: June 29, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- The Army went against the wishes of Delta Junction residents when it chose a site to build a military training complex, officials have acknowledged. The 22,000-acre Eddy Drop Zone was selected over two sites that are farther from town, said Maj. Ben Danner. The chosen site is 2.2 miles from the closest home, 4.1 miles from a school on Fort Greely and 7.6 miles from the Delta Junction school, Danner said. He added that the decision was based on environmental and operational factors, construction considerations and community concerns about the area. "There's just a staggering number of considerations that we look at in o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Kristine Bernaldo of the Filipino Altaptap Folk Art Dancers watches as the Filipino Community Manila Square is dedicated Saturday in downtown Juneau. The city passed a proclamation to dedicate the corner where South Franklin Street, Marine Way and Admiral Way come together as Manila Way. Juneau Filipino Community Inc. erected a statue bust of Dr. Jose P. Rizal, the Philippines National Hero, at the site, and a proclamation from Gov. Frank Murkowski named June 21 as Filipino Community Recognition Day in Alaska. The event included dancing, singing, and a feast of traditional Filipino food. The Square is the first of its kind in Alaska. (Photo by DAVID SHEAKLEY / The Associated Press) Published: June 25, 2003 • Back to main story • Go to Photo Galleries

Rick Foster, left, chief executive of ASRC Management Services, said he and Craciun are scouting for opportunities in our nation's capital. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Avoid career roadblocks by developing talents Lynne Curry management (Published: June 23, 2003) Q. I just moved to this town to marry a man I met at a national conference. Because only three companies in town hire employees with my type of work experience, I applied for work with all three and that's when everything started to fall apart. The first two companies turned me down because my husband works for one of their competitors. Today I got the news that I got turned down at my husband's company because of a nepotism policy preventing them from hiring spouses or family members for closely allied positions or positions in which one family member currently supervises or will potentially supervise another. I'm devastated. I've invested eight years of my life in developing my profession …

summary: In Alaska Outdoors News More headlines » Alaska | National | World | Business | Sports Life jackets more vital than any seat belt Craig medred Daily News Outdoor (Published: June 1, 2003) With a meager $15,000 to spend on advertising, Alaska Boating Safety director Jeff Johnson is waging a battle to end the state's long-standing position as the national leader in boating deaths per capita. He faces more than a few hurdles, including the myth that water in Alaska is so cold you will die in minutes, if not seconds, should you fall in. Some cite this misconception as the reason they avoid life jackets. Why spend the money on a float coat if all it does is make it easier for someone to recover your body? The problem with this thinking is that even in Alaska's coldest water, a PFD can prolong l…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Most Alaska salmon fishing is not a solitary pursuit (Published: June 29, 2003) Anglers line the banks of the Russian River while fishing for red salmon. When the reds are in the Russian, it generally means combat fishing. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News archives 2000) Click on photo to enlarge KIJIK RIVER -- Archaeologists say the delta of this tributary to Lake Clark was once home to what was probably the largest Native community in Alaska. The people gathered here, of course, for the salmon. All of which leaves one to wonder if their polemics wrung their hands over the "combat'' nature of so many gathering for the kill. How could the commentators of the day have avoided it? Just think of the distasteful sight of early Alaskans standing shoulder to shoulder, spears in hand…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Josh Fink to defend against state power ADVOCATE: New job is to protect the most vulnerable. By LISA DEMER Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 24, 2003) Josh Fink has been appointed Director of the State Office of Public Advocacy. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Sure, says Josh Fink, he's been accused of being a "liberal" Republican. But the state's new top attorney for the most vulnerable Alaskans says he doesn't see himself that way. "I don't know that that fits because I am a fiscal conservative," said Fink, who will become director of the state Office of Public Advocacy on July 1. summary: In Alaska Business News More headlines » Alaska | National | World | Sports Interior's tourism industry optimistic as first visitors begin to arrive The Associated Press (Published: June 6, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- Dan Archuleta and Jacquelyn Poulin are nomads traveling in style. They traded their ocean-view California home for a new RV and headed north. "We just sold everything and decided to quit work and take some time for ourselves," Poulin said. They are living at Santaland RV Park in North Pole, visiting hot springs and thoroughly enjoying their first Alaska adventure. Poulin, 56, and Archuleta, 51, are just two of the thousands of expected visitors who will be exploring the Interior this summer as the tourist season gears up. No one can predict exactly how many people will head north, but…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Sheriff: spay/neuter clinic canceled, agreement reached The Associated Press GREAT FALLS, Mont. (June 25, 9:55 a.m. ADT) - Toole County and the American Working Collie Association have reached an agreement to spay and neuter about 145 rescued collies at area veterinary offices, rather than at a one-day clinic planned for this weekend. Toole County Sheriff Donna Matoon and AWCA President Jean Levitt said Wednesday that the AWCA, which strongly opposed the one-day clinic, would pay to have the operations performed at veterinarian offices instead. "We have canceled the project on Saturday," Matoon said. "Instead, the AWCA is going to pay to send small groups of dogs to vet clinics." Van Etten () Click on photo to enlarge At the hearing Thursday afternoon, Van Etten was apologetic and made no attempt to evade the violations. "There's no justifiable excuse," he said. Tony Knowles, will take the same job with Mayor-elect Mark Begich. Dennis Fradley, who was spokesman for Mayor George Wuerch, took over Gov. Frank Murkowski's public relations. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Six rafters dumped in river SUSITNA: Raft hits log and tips; all aboard wore flotation devices. Daily News staff (Published: June 15, 2003) Six rafters were dumped into knee-deep water Saturday afternoon when a Talkeetna River Guides boat flipped on a channel of the Susitna River near Trapper Creek, according to one of the company's owners. No one was seriously injured, said Margaret Salmon, a co-owner and local medic. The raft, which was floating the river with another raft, hit a log on a narrow, flat stretch of the river and tipped sideways, dumping the rafters in the water, Salmon said. Everybody aboard was wearing a flotation device, she said. The guide righted the raft and, with the other raft, pulled people out of the water, Salmon said. Some clients also pulled themselves onto …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Seniors' aid depends on low income GUIDELINES: State expects 7,500 to qualify; 18,000 got longevity bonus. By CATHY BROWN The Associated Press (Published: June 27, 2003) JUNEAU -- The state announced income guidelines Thursday for a new program to provide $120 monthly payments for low-income seniors who will lose longevity bonuses in September. Senior advocates said the program will help some needy senior citizens, but they fear others will fall through the cracks. "As my mother used to say, it's better than a sharp stick in the eye," said Pat Luby, legislative representative for AARP in Alaska. "It's still going to be a mess for many of our oldest citizens." Senior citizens living alone can qualify for the new program if they make no more than $15,134 a year. Couples can make up to $2…
